rows.llm_rationale: As of May 2026, ~7 months remain until year-end. Current US economic data would be critical—the crowd price of 24% appears slightly underpriced given typical recession base rates (~15-20% annually) and the 7-month remaining window. Recent Fed policy, labor market strength, and yield curve conditions should drive material divergence from historical base rates. Without real-time 2026 data, a modest upward adjustment to 28% accounts for accumulated recession risk over the extended horizon while respecting the crowd's signal.
rows.question: US recession by end of 2026?

rows.llm_rationale: As of May 2026, with ~7 months remaining in the year, the crowd price of 17.5% appears slightly underpriced relative to baseline Fed cutting probability. Historical rate-cut frequency and current economic conditions (assuming typical late-cycle dynamics) suggest at least one cut by year-end is moderately likely. CME FedWatch data would be the reference benchmark, but absent specific real-time data, a modest upward adjustment from the crowd price to ~22% reflects reasonable probability while maintaining calibration.
rows.question: Will 1 Fed rate cut happen in 2026?
